items.title: Gravity near Earth
items.fact: Near Earth's surface, the acceleration due to gravity is about 9.8 m/s^2 downward. If a student asks for the force of gravity, clarify that force depends on mass.
items.formula: g = 9.8 m/s^2; weight force Fg = m × g
examples: A 10 kg object has weight Fg = 10 kg × 9.8 m/s^2 = 98 N.

items.title: Mass
items.fact: Mass is the amount of matter in an object. Mass is measured in grams or kilograms and does not change just because gravity changes.
items.formula: mass can be found from m = F/a or m = density × volume when those values are known

items.title: Weight
items.fact: Weight is the force of gravity pulling on an object. Weight changes when gravity changes and is measured in newtons.

items.title: Matter
items.fact: Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space.

items.title: Density
items.fact: Density tells how much mass is packed into a certain volume. Common units are g/mL, g/cm^3, or kg/m^3.
items.formula: density = mass / volume; D = m / V
examples: If mass = 20 g and volume = 5 cm^3, density = 20 / 5 = 4 g/cm^3.

items.title: Speed
items.fact: Speed tells how fast an object moves. It does not include direction.
items.formula: speed = distance / time; s = d / t
examples: If distance = 100 m and time = 20 s, speed = 5 m/s.

items.title: Velocity
items.fact: Velocity is speed in a specific direction. A car moving 20 m/s north has a velocity because direction is included.

items.title: Acceleration
items.fact: Acceleration is the rate that velocity changes over time. It can mean speeding up, slowing down, or changing direction.
items.formula: acceleration = change in velocity / time; a = Δv / t
examples: If velocity changes from 0 m/s to 20 m/s in 5 s, acceleration = 4 m/s^2.

items.title: Newton's Second Law
items.fact: Newton's Second Law says an object's acceleration depends on the net force on the object and the object's mass.
examples: If mass = 3 kg and acceleration = 2 m/s^2, net force = 6 N.

items.title: Newton's First Law
items.fact: Newton's First Law says an object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at the same speed and direction, unless acted on by an unbalanced force.

items.title: Newton's Third Law
items.fact: Newton's Third Law says that for every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force.

items.title: Momentum
items.fact: Momentum describes how hard it is to stop a moving object. More mass or more velocity means more momentum.
examples: If mass = 4 kg and velocity = 3 m/s, momentum = 12 kg·m/s.

items.title: Kinetic Energy
items.fact: Kinetic energy is the energy an object has because it is moving.
examples: If mass = 2 kg and velocity = 3 m/s, KE = 0.5 × 2 × 9 = 9 J.

items.title: Gravitational Potential Energy
items.fact: Gravitational potential energy is stored energy based on an object's mass, gravity, and height.
examples: If mass = 2 kg, g = 9.8 m/s^2, and height = 5 m, GPE = 98 J.

items.title: Power
items.fact: Power is how quickly work is done or energy is transferred.
items.formula: power = work / time; P = W / t

items.title: Work
items.fact: In physics, work happens when a force moves an object over a distance in the direction of the force.
items.formula: work = force × distance; W = F × d

items.title: Ohm's Law
items.fact: Ohm's Law shows the relationship between voltage, current, and resistance in a circuit.
examples: If current = 2 A and resistance = 6 Ω, voltage = 12 V.

items.title: Atom
items.fact: An atom is the smallest unit of an element that still has that element's properties. Atoms contain protons, neutrons, and electrons.

items.title: Proton
items.fact: A proton is a positively charged particle in the nucleus of an atom. The number of protons tells the element's atomic number and identity.

items.title: Neutron
terms: what charge does a neutron have
items.fact: A neutron is a particle in the nucleus with no electric charge. Protons plus neutrons make the mass number.

items.title: Electron
terms: what charge does an electron have
items.fact: An electron is a negatively charged particle found outside the nucleus. Valence electrons are the outer electrons involved in bonding.

items.title: Ion
items.fact: An ion is an atom or group of atoms with a charge because it gained or lost electrons. Losing electrons makes a positive ion; gaining electrons makes a negative ion.

items.title: Isotope
items.fact: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.

items.title: Periodic Table Groups and Periods
items.fact: Groups are vertical columns on the periodic table. Periods are horizontal rows. Elements in the same group often have similar properties because they have the same number of valence electrons.

items.title: Common Element Quick Reference
items.fact: Common element symbols and atomic numbers: Hydrogen H=1, Helium He=2, Carbon C=6, Nitrogen N=7, Oxygen O=8, Sodium Na=11, Chlorine Cl=17, Iron Fe=26, Copper Cu=29, Silver Ag=47, Gold Au=79, Lead Pb=82.

items.title: Atomic Number
items.fact: The atomic number tells the number of protons in an atom. The atomic number is used to identify the element. In a neutral atom, the number of protons equals the number of electrons.
examples: Carbon has atomic number 6, so every carbon atom has 6 protons.

items.title: Chemical Symbol
items.fact: A chemical symbol is the abbreviation used to represent an element on the periodic table.
examples: H is hydrogen, O is oxygen, Na is sodium, and Cl is chlorine.

items.title: Mass Number
items.fact: Mass number tells the total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus of an atom. Most of an atom's mass is found in the nucleus.
examples: If an atom has 11 protons and 12 neutrons, its mass number is 23.

items.title: Finding Neutrons
items.fact: To find the number of neutrons, subtract the atomic number from the mass number. The atomic number is the number of protons.
examples: Potassium-40 has 19 protons, so it has 40 - 19 = 21 neutrons.

items.title: Electrons in a Neutral Atom
items.fact: In an electrically neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons.
examples: A neutral oxygen atom has 8 protons and 8 electrons.

items.title: Nucleus
items.fact: The nucleus is the dense center of the atom. It contains protons and neutrons, has a positive charge, and contains most of the atom's mass.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Structure of the Atom.pptx, slide 7

items.title: Electron Cloud
items.fact: The electron cloud is the space around the nucleus where electrons are found. It is divided into energy levels or shells. Electrons farther from the nucleus have more energy.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Structure of the Atom.pptx, slide 7

items.title: Valence Electrons
items.fact: Valence electrons are the electrons in the outermost energy level of an atom. They are important because they help determine how an atom bonds with other atoms.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Structure of the Atom.pptx, slide 7

items.title: Atomic Model History
terms: democritus dalton thomson rutherford bohr electron cloud
items.fact: Atomic models changed over time as scientists gathered more evidence. Democritus named the atom, Dalton described atoms as solid spheres, Thomson proposed the plum pudding model, Rutherford discovered the nucleus, Bohr described electrons in fixed energy levels, and the electron cloud model describes electrons in regions of space instead of fixed orbits.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Structure of the Atom.pptx, slides 4-6

items.title: Isotope Notation
items.fact: Isotopes can be written in hyphen form or nuclear form. Hyphen form uses the element name and mass number. Nuclear form places the mass number before the chemical symbol.
examples: Potassium-40 and 40K both mean potassium with a mass number of 40.
Boron-11 can be written as 11B.

items.title: Average Atomic Mass
items.fact: Average atomic mass is the weighted average of all the naturally occurring isotopes of an element. It is closest to the most common isotope.
examples: Hydrogen's average atomic mass is about 1.01, so the most common isotope is hydrogen-1.

items.title: Periodic Table
items.fact: The periodic table organizes all known elements by atomic number and chemical properties.

items.title: Groups and Valence Electrons
items.fact: Groups are vertical columns on the periodic table. Elements in the same group have the same number of valence electrons, which is why they often have similar chemical properties.
examples: Group 1 elements have 1 valence electron. Group 2 elements have 2 valence electrons. Group 17 elements have 7 valence electrons. Group 18 elements usually have 8 valence electrons, except helium.

items.title: Periods and Energy Levels
items.fact: Periods are horizontal rows on the periodic table. Elements in the same period have the same number of energy levels in their electron cloud.
examples: Magnesium is in period 3, so it has 3 energy levels.

items.title: Periodic Table Group Names
items.fact: Important periodic table group names include Group 1 alkali metals, Group 2 alkaline earth metals, Groups 3-12 transition metals, Group 17 halogens, and Group 18 noble gases. Noble gases are mostly nonreactive because they are already stable.

items.title: Metal Reactivity Trend
items.fact: For metals, reactivity generally increases as you move down a group. Group 1 alkali metals are the most reactive metals, not including hydrogen.

items.title: Nonmetal Reactivity Trend
items.fact: For nonmetals, reactivity generally decreases as you move down a group. Group 17 halogens are the most reactive nonmetals.

items.title: Metals
items.fact: Metals are usually shiny, silvery solids that conduct heat and electricity well. They are malleable and ductile and are located to the left of the metalloids on the periodic table.

items.title: Nonmetals
items.fact: Nonmetals may be gases or dull, brittle solids. They are poor conductors and are found to the right of the metalloids, with hydrogen as an exception.

items.title: Metalloids
items.fact: Metalloids are solids and semiconductors. They have physical properties like metals but chemical properties like nonmetals.

items.title: Bohr Model
items.fact: A Bohr model is a simple diagram that shows the atomic structure of an atom. It shows protons and neutrons in the nucleus and electrons placed in energy levels around the nucleus.

items.title: How to Draw a Bohr Model
items.fact: To draw a Bohr model, use the atomic number to find protons, subtract atomic number from mass number to find neutrons, place protons and neutrons in the nucleus, use the period number to find energy levels, then place electrons from the inside energy level outward. For a neutral atom, electrons equal protons.
examples: If mass number is not given, round the average atomic mass to estimate the most common isotope.

items.title: Basic Energy Level Capacity
terms: how many electrons in each shell
items.fact: For this physical science class, the first energy level holds up to 2 electrons, the second holds up to 8, the third holds up to 8, and the fourth holds up to 18.

items.title: Compound
terms: two or more elements chemically combined
items.fact: A compound is a substance made of two or more different elements chemically combined. The properties of a compound can be very different from the properties of the elements that make it.
examples: Sodium and chlorine have very different properties from sodium chloride, NaCl.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slide 2

items.title: Chemical Formula
items.fact: A chemical formula tells which elements are in a compound and how many atoms of each element are in one unit of the compound.
examples: H2O contains 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom.
NH3 contains 1 nitrogen atom and 3 hydrogen atoms.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slide 3

items.title: Octet Rule
items.fact: The octet rule says atoms gain, lose, or share electrons to have 8 electrons in their outer energy level. Hydrogen and helium are exceptions because they are stable with 2 electrons.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slide 4

items.title: Chemical Bond
items.fact: A chemical bond is a force that holds atoms together in a substance. Atoms form bonds by gaining, losing, or sharing electrons to become more stable.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slide 8

items.title: Noble Gases and Stability
items.fact: Noble gases are mostly nonreactive because their outermost energy levels are already full, making them chemically stable.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slide 6

items.title: Ionic Bond
items.fact: An ionic bond forms when electrons are transferred from one atom to another so the atoms become stable. Ionic bonds usually form between a metal and a nonmetal and result in the formation of ions.
examples: Sodium loses 1 electron and chlorine gains 1 electron to form NaCl.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slide 8

items.title: Cation
items.fact: A cation is a positively charged ion. Cations form when atoms lose electrons. Metals usually form cations.
examples: Magnesium loses 2 electrons to form Mg+2.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slides 9-10

items.title: Anion
items.fact: An anion is a negatively charged ion. Anions form when atoms gain electrons. Nonmetals usually form anions.
examples: Oxygen gains 2 electrons to form O-2.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slides 11-12

items.title: Oxidation Number
items.fact: An oxidation number is the charge of an ion in a compound. It is written as a superscript to the right of the chemical symbol.
examples: Group 1 forms +1 ions, Group 2 forms +2 ions, Group 13 forms +3 ions, Group 15 forms -3 ions, Group 16 forms -2 ions, and Group 17 forms -1 ions.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slide 16

items.title: Common Ion Charges by Group
items.fact: For main-group elements, common ion charges can be predicted from the group number. Group 1 forms +1, Group 2 forms +2, Group 13 forms +3, Group 14 can be plus or minus 4, Group 15 forms -3, Group 16 forms -2, and Group 17 forms -1. Skip transition metals because their charges can vary.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slide 16

items.title: Lewis Dot Diagram
items.fact: A Lewis dot diagram, also called an electron dot diagram, shows the valence electrons of an atom as dots around the chemical symbol.
examples: Use the group number to find the number of valence electrons, then draw that many dots around the symbol.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slides 17-19

items.title: Using Electron Dot Diagrams for Bonding
terms: use lewis dot to form compound
items.fact: To use electron dot diagrams for ionic bonding, draw the electron dot structures, transfer electrons from the metal to the nonmetal, add atoms as needed until all atoms are stable, then write the chemical formula using subscripts.
examples: Potassium and chlorine form KCl.
Magnesium and fluorine form MgF2.
Aluminum and nitrogen form AlN.
Magnesium and phosphorus form Mg3P2.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Stability and Bonding.pptx, slides 21-26

items.title: Ionic Compound
items.fact: An ionic compound is made of charged ions. The metal forms a positive cation and the nonmetal forms a negative anion. The overall charge of the compound must be zero.
items.source: Concept 2 Notes - Naming Ionic Compounds.pptx, slide 3

items.title: Writing Ionic Formulas
items.fact: To write an ionic formula, write the ions with their charges, crisscross the charges to become subscripts, rewrite the formula with subscripts, and reduce or simplify if needed. The final compound must have an overall charge of zero.
examples: Magnesium and phosphorus form Mg3P2 because 3(+2) + 2(-3) = 0.
Calcium and oxygen form CaO, not Ca2O2, because the formula is reduced.
items.source: Concept 2 Notes - Naming Ionic Compounds.pptx, slides 4-5

items.title: Naming Ionic Compounds
items.fact: To name a basic ionic compound, write the name of the metal first. Then write the name of the nonmetal with an -ide ending.
examples: CaS is calcium sulfide.
MgCl2 is magnesium chloride.
Na2O is sodium oxide.
items.source: Concept 2 Notes - Naming Ionic Compounds.pptx, slide 7

items.title: Polyatomic Ion
items.fact: A polyatomic ion is a positively or negatively charged group of covalently bonded atoms. When naming or writing formulas, keep the polyatomic ion together and use its special name.
items.source: Concept 2 Notes - Naming Ionic Compounds.pptx, slide 10

items.title: Common Polyatomic Ions
terms: ammonium sulfate nitrate carbonate hydroxide acetate phosphate chlorate
items.fact: Common polyatomic ions to know: ammonium NH4+1, nitrate NO3-1, hydroxide OH-1, acetate C2H3O2-1, sulfate SO4-2, carbonate CO3-2, phosphate PO4-3, and chlorate ClO3-1.
items.source: Concept 2 Notes - Naming Ionic Compounds.pptx, slide 10

items.title: Naming Ionic Compounds with Polyatomic Ions
items.fact: When naming ionic compounds with polyatomic ions, use the special name of the polyatomic ion. Do not change a polyatomic ion to an -ide ending.
examples: Na3PO4 is sodium phosphate.
MgSO4 is magnesium sulfate.
NH4Cl is ammonium chloride.
items.source: Concept 2 Notes - Naming Ionic Compounds.pptx, slide 11

items.title: Writing Formulas with Polyatomic Ions
items.fact: When writing formulas with polyatomic ions, keep the polyatomic ion together. Use parentheses if more than one of the polyatomic ion is needed. Do not change anything inside the polyatomic ion.
examples: Calcium phosphate is Ca3(PO4)2.
Calcium nitrate is Ca(NO3)2.
Aluminum hydroxide is Al(OH)3.
items.source: Concept 2 Notes - Naming Ionic Compounds.pptx, slide 13

items.title: Transition Metals and Roman Numerals
items.fact: Transition metals can form more than one ion charge, so a Roman numeral is used in the compound name to show the charge of the metal.
examples: CuCl is copper (I) chloride.
CuCl2 is copper (II) chloride.
FeSO4 is iron (II) sulfate.
items.source: Concept 2 Notes - Naming Ionic Compounds.pptx, slides 16-17

items.title: Finding a Transition Metal Charge
items.fact: To find the Roman numeral for a transition metal, use the known charge of the nonmetal or polyatomic ion and determine what metal charge makes the total compound charge zero. Reverse crisscross can help, but always double check because formulas may have been simplified.
examples: In Sn3(PO4)4, phosphate is -3. Four phosphates give -12 total, so three tin ions must give +12 total. Each tin is +4, so the name is tin (IV) phosphate.
In FeSO4, sulfate is -2, so iron must be +2. The name is iron (II) sulfate.
items.source: Concept 2 Notes - Naming Ionic Compounds.pptx, slides 18-19

items.title: Covalent Bond
items.fact: A covalent bond forms when nonmetal atoms share electrons. Covalent compounds are usually made of nonmetals.
items.source: Concept 3 Notes - Naming Covalent Compounds.pptx, slide 2

items.title: Molecule
items.fact: A molecule is a compound formed when two or more elements are covalently bonded.
items.source: Concept 3 Notes - Naming Covalent Compounds.pptx, slide 2

items.title: Single, Double, and Triple Bonds
items.fact: Covalent bonds can be single, double, or triple. A single bond shares 2 electrons, a double bond shares 4 electrons, and a triple bond shares 6 electrons.
examples: H2 has a single bond.
O2 has a double bond.
N2 has a triple bond.
items.source: Concept 3 Notes - Naming Covalent Compounds.pptx, slides 2-5

items.title: Properties of Covalent Compounds
items.fact: Covalent compounds are made of nonmetals sharing electrons. They form molecules that can be solids, liquids, or gases. They usually have low melting and boiling points and do not conduct electricity when dissolved in water.
items.source: Concept 3 Notes - Naming Covalent Compounds.pptx, slide 6

items.title: Properties of Ionic Compounds
items.fact: Ionic compounds are made of positive metal ions and negative nonmetal ions. They are arranged in regular repeating patterns called crystals, usually form solids, have high melting and boiling points, and can conduct electricity when dissolved in water.
items.source: Concept 3 Notes - Naming Covalent Compounds.pptx, slide 7

items.title: Covalent Prefixes
items.fact: Covalent compound names use prefixes to show the number of atoms. The prefixes are mono = 1, di = 2, tri = 3, tetra = 4, penta = 5, hexa = 6, hepta = 7, octa = 8, nona = 9, and deca = 10.
items.source: Concept 3 Notes - Naming Covalent Compounds.pptx, slide 9

items.title: Naming Covalent Compounds
items.fact: To name a covalent compound, name the first element with a prefix to show how many atoms there are, but do not use mono- for one atom on the first element. Name the second element with a prefix and change the ending to -ide.
examples: CO2 is carbon dioxide.
PCl3 is phosphorus trichloride.
P4O10 is tetraphosphorus decaoxide.
items.source: Concept 3 Notes - Naming Covalent Compounds.pptx, slides 8-10

items.title: Writing Covalent Formulas
items.fact: To write a covalent formula, use the prefixes in the name to determine the subscripts. Do not crisscross charges or simplify covalent formulas.
examples: Dichlorine monoxide is Cl2O.
Sulfur pentoxide is SO5.
Silicon tetrafluoride is SiF4.
items.source: Concept 3 Notes - Naming Covalent Compounds.pptx, slides 11-12

items.title: Ionic vs Covalent Compounds
items.fact: Ionic compounds usually form between a metal and a nonmetal when electrons are transferred. Covalent compounds usually form between nonmetals when electrons are shared.
items.source: Concept 2 Notes - Naming Ionic Compounds.pptx, slide 2; Concept 3 Notes - Naming Covalent Compounds.pptx, slide 2

items.title: Wave
items.fact: A wave is a rhythmic disturbance that transfers energy through matter or space. A wave carries energy without carrying matter from place to place.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 2

items.title: Medium
items.fact: A medium is matter that a wave travels through. A medium can be a solid, liquid, or gas.
examples: Sound waves and water waves travel through a medium.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 3

items.title: Electromagnetic vs Mechanical Waves
items.fact: Electromagnetic waves can transfer energy through a vacuum and do not need a medium. Mechanical waves can only travel through a medium.
examples: Light is an electromagnetic wave. Sound is a mechanical wave.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 3

items.title: Transverse Waves
items.fact: In a transverse wave, matter in the medium moves back and forth at right angles to the direction the wave travels. Particle motion is perpendicular to wave motion.
examples: Electromagnetic waves, vibrations in string instruments, and ripples on the surface of water are examples.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 4

items.title: Longitudinal Waves
items.fact: In a longitudinal wave, matter in the medium moves back and forth in the same direction the wave travels. Particle motion is parallel to wave motion.
examples: Sound waves, ultrasounds, and waves made with a slinky are examples.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 4

items.title: Crest and Trough
items.fact: In a transverse wave, the crest is the highest point of the wave and the trough is the lowest point of the wave.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 5

items.title: Compression and Rarefaction
items.fact: In a longitudinal wave, a compression is where particles are pushed together and a rarefaction is where particles are spread apart.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 6

items.title: Amplitude
terms: amount of energy carried by a wave
items.fact: Amplitude is the amount of energy carried by a wave. In transverse waves, amplitude is shown by the height of the wave. In longitudinal waves, amplitude is shown by the amount of compression.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 7

items.title: Wavelength
items.fact: Wavelength is the distance between one point on a wave and the nearest point just like it. It is measured in meters.
examples: In transverse waves, wavelength can be measured crest to crest or trough to trough. In longitudinal waves, it can be measured compression to compression or rarefaction to rarefaction.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 8

items.title: Period
items.fact: Period is the amount of time it takes one wavelength to pass a point. Period is measured in seconds.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 9

items.title: Frequency
items.fact: Frequency is the number of waves that pass a given point in one second. Frequency is measured in Hertz, or Hz.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 9

items.title: Frequency and Period Relationship
items.fact: Frequency and period have an inverse relationship. If frequency increases, period decreases. If period increases, frequency decreases.
examples: If frequency is 4 Hz, period is 1 / 4 = 0.25 s.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 9

items.title: Frequency and Wavelength Relationship
items.fact: Frequency and wavelength have an inverse relationship. Higher frequency means shorter wavelength. Lower frequency means longer wavelength.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 10

items.title: Wave Speed
items.fact: Wave speed tells how fast a wave travels. It is measured in meters per second. Wave speed depends on the type of wave and the properties of the medium it travels through.
items.formula: v = wavelength x frequency; v = lambda x f
examples: If wavelength = 2 m and frequency = 3 Hz, wave speed = 2 x 3 = 6 m/s.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slides 11-12

items.title: Finding Frequency from Wave Speed
items.fact: To find frequency when wave speed and wavelength are known, divide wave speed by wavelength.
examples: If a wave travels at 12 m/s and has a wavelength of 3 m, frequency = 12 / 3 = 4 Hz.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 13

items.title: Wave Speed and Medium
items.fact: Mechanical waves, like sound, travel fastest through solids, then liquids, then gases because particles are closer together. Mechanical waves also travel faster through warmer mediums. Electromagnetic waves, like light, travel fastest through a vacuum and slow down when traveling through matter.
items.source: Concept 1 Notes - Nature and Properties.pptx, slide 11

items.title: Reflection
items.fact: Reflection happens when a wave strikes an object and bounces off of it. All types of waves can be reflected.
examples: An echo is a reflected sound wave.

items.title: Law of Reflection
items.fact: The law of reflection says the angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection. The incident ray is the wave that strikes the surface. The reflected ray is the wave that bounces off the surface.

items.title: Refraction
items.fact: Refraction is the bending of waves caused by a change in speed as the waves move from one medium into another. The greater the change in speed, the more the wave bends.

items.title: Prisms and Rainbows
items.fact: A prism separates white light because light slows down and refracts when it enters a new medium. Different colors of light have different wavelengths, so they bend different amounts and separate into colors.
examples: Rainbows happen because light is separated into colors.

items.title: Diffraction
items.fact: Diffraction happens when an object causes a wave to change direction and bend around it. The amount of diffraction depends on the size of the object or opening and the wavelength of the wave.
examples: Sound waves tend to diffract better than light waves because sound waves are larger.

items.title: Absorption
items.fact: Absorption happens when a wave strikes an object and goes into it. The energy from the wave is converted to thermal energy.
examples: Dark colors absorb light better than light colors, which is why dark clothing can feel warmer in the winter.

items.title: Interference
items.fact: Interference happens when two or more waves combine to form a new wave.

items.title: Constructive Interference
items.fact: Constructive interference happens when two or more waves add together. Like parts of waves line up, causing an increase in amplitude.

items.title: Destructive Interference
items.fact: Destructive interference happens when two or more waves subtract from one another when they overlap. Opposite parts of waves line up.

items.title: Electromagnetic Waves
items.fact: Electromagnetic waves are electric and magnetic waves released by a vibrating electric charge. They can transfer energy through a vacuum and do not need a medium. All electromagnetic waves travel at 300,000,000 m/s in a vacuum and slow down when traveling through matter.

items.title: Photons
items.fact: Electromagnetic waves can behave as waves and as particles called photons. A photon is a massless bundle of energy.

items.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
items.fact: Electromagnetic waves give off energy in the form of electromagnetic radiation.

items.title: Electromagnetic Spectrum
items.fact: The electromagnetic spectrum is the entire range of frequencies of electromagnetic radiation.

items.title: EM Spectrum Energy, Wavelength, and Frequency
items.fact: On the electromagnetic spectrum, longer wavelength means lower frequency and lower energy. Shorter wavelength means higher frequency and higher energy.

items.title: Visible Light and ROYGBIV
items.fact: Visible light is the only part of the electromagnetic spectrum humans can see. Different colors of light have different wavelengths. In ROYGBIV, red has the longest wavelength and violet has the shortest wavelength.

items.title: Radio Waves
items.fact: Radio waves have the longest wavelength and lowest frequency on the electromagnetic spectrum. They are used by radio stations, MRI, RADAR, Wi-Fi, TV, and cell phones.

items.title: Microwaves
items.fact: Microwaves are electromagnetic waves used for cooking, Doppler radar, and GPS.
examples: Doppler radar is used for weather forecasting.

items.title: Infrared Waves
items.fact: Infrared waves are electromagnetic waves used in night vision goggles, remote controls, and infrared photography.

items.title: Ultraviolet Waves
items.fact: Ultraviolet waves are given off by the sun. They are used in some light bulbs, to kill bacteria, and by the human body to make vitamin D.

items.title: X-Rays
items.fact: X-rays are electromagnetic waves used to take pictures of bones to check for breaks and fractures. They are also used in CAT scans. X-rays can penetrate skin cells, but overexposure can cause cancer.

items.title: Gamma Rays
items.fact: Gamma rays have the smallest wavelength and highest frequency on the electromagnetic spectrum. They are used to kill cancer cells, sterilize medical equipment, and in radioactive tracers.

items.title: Static Electricity
items.fact: Static electricity is a charge imbalance that results in the buildup of excess electric charge on an object. Shock happens when the charge is neutralized.
examples: A shock after walking on carpet can happen because charge built up and then moved suddenly.

items.title: Charge Rules
items.fact: Opposite electric charges attract. Like electric charges repel.

items.title: Law of Conservation of Charge
terms: charge cannot be created or destroyed
items.fact: The Law of Conservation of Charge says charge cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred. Net electric charge is always constant.

items.title: Electric Field
items.fact: An electric field is the area surrounding charged particles or objects where a force can be exerted on other charged particles or objects. Electric fields allow charges to act at a distance.

items.title: Electroscope
items.fact: An electroscope is a tool used to detect the presence and magnitude of an electric charge.

items.title: Conductors
terms: materials that allow electrons to flow
items.fact: Conductors are materials that allow electrons to flow easily and readily conduct electric charge.
examples: Metals, especially copper in wires, and skin are examples of conductors.

items.title: Insulators
items.fact: Insulators are materials that block the flow of electrons through them.
examples: Plastic, wood, air, rubber, and glass are examples of insulators.

items.title: Charging by Conduction
items.fact: Conduction is the transfer of electrons through direct contact between objects. Electrons move from one object to another object, and the object must be a conductor to transfer charge well.

items.title: Charging by Friction
items.fact: Friction is the transfer of electrons through rubbing objects together.
examples: Socks rubbing on carpet can build up charge, which can lead to a shock when touching another object.

items.title: Charging by Induction
items.fact: Induction is the transfer or movement of electrons caused by electric fields working over a distance. A charged object brought near a neutral object can cause polarization and induce a charge on the neutral object.
examples: A charged balloon can stick to a wall because it induces an opposite charge on the wall's surface.

items.title: Static Discharge
items.fact: Static discharge is the sudden flow of electric charge between two charged objects because of a buildup of static electricity.
examples: Lightning is an example of static discharge.

items.title: Lightning and Static Electricity
items.fact: Movement of clouds and air currents can cause a buildup of static electricity in clouds. When enough electrons build up, a positive charge is induced on Earth, and electrons can travel from the clouds to Earth to neutralize the charge imbalance.

items.title: Grounding
items.fact: Grounding is the process of removing excess charge on an object by transferring electrons to another object to neutralize the electrical imbalance. It redirects charge to the ground rather than a building.

items.title: Electric Current
items.fact: Electric current is the net movement of electric charge in a single direction. Current is represented by I and is measured in amperes, or amps.

items.title: Voltage Difference
terms: push that causes charge to move
items.fact: Voltage difference is the push or force that causes charge to move. It is measured in volts. Charge flows from high voltage areas to low voltage areas, and a larger voltage difference creates a greater current.

items.title: Circuit
items.fact: A circuit is a closed path that electric current flows through. A circuit is necessary for electric charge to move. If the switch is open, current will not flow. If the switch is closed, current will flow.

items.title: Battery
items.fact: A battery is a device that converts chemical energy to electrical energy. Chemical reactions create a voltage difference between the positive and negative terminals. When the terminals are connected, current flows through the conductor because of the voltage difference. Electrons flow from the negative end to the positive end.

items.title: Resistance
items.fact: Resistance is the tendency of a material to oppose the flow of electrons through it. Resistance is represented by R and is measured in ohms. Resistance is affected by material, wire diameter, wire length, and temperature.
examples: Insulators have more resistance than conductors. Smaller diameter wire, longer wire, and higher temperature all increase resistance.

items.title: Ohm's Law Expanded
items.fact: Ohm's Law shows the relationship between voltage difference, current, and resistance. Current is directly proportional to voltage difference. Higher voltage means higher current, and lower resistance means higher current.
examples: If current = 0.75 A and resistance = 10 ohms, voltage = 0.75 x 10 = 7.5 V.

items.title: Series Circuit
items.fact: A series circuit has only one path for current to travel through. It is simpler, all components must work at the same time, adding more items increases overall resistance, voltage decreases as it travels through the circuit, and current stays the same throughout.
examples: Flashlights and some holiday lights are examples of series circuits.

items.title: Parallel Circuit
items.fact: A parallel circuit has more than one path for current to travel through. It is more complex, can use one component at a time, adding more items decreases total resistance, voltage stays the same throughout, and current is split among each pathway.
examples: Houses and schools use parallel circuits.

items.title: Combination Circuit
items.fact: Some circuits are combination circuits, meaning they include both series and parallel parts.

items.title: Circuit Symbols
items.fact: Circuit diagrams use symbols for parts such as switches, batteries, light bulbs, resistors, ammeters, and voltmeters. A closed switch allows current to flow, while an open switch stops current.

items.title: Ammeter
items.fact: An ammeter measures the amount of current in a circuit. In this class, ammeters are placed in series circuits.

items.title: Voltmeter
items.fact: A voltmeter measures voltage drop. In this class, voltmeters are placed in parallel circuits.

items.title: Fuse
items.fact: A fuse is a small piece of metal that melts when overheated. This opens the circuit and prevents current from flowing.

items.title: Circuit Breaker
items.fact: A circuit breaker uses a piece of metal that bends when overheated. This opens the circuit and prevents current from flowing.

items.title: Total Resistance in Series
items.fact: In a series circuit, total resistance is found by adding all resistances together.
examples: If a series circuit has 2 ohms and 5 ohms of resistance, Rt = 2 + 5 = 7 ohms.

items.title: Total Resistance in Parallel
items.fact: In a parallel circuit, total resistance is found by adding the reciprocals of each resistance, then solving for total resistance. Total resistance in parallel is less than the resistance of any single branch.
examples: For parallel resistors of 2 ohms and 5 ohms, 1/Rt = 1/2 + 1/5 = 7/10, so Rt = 10/7 = 1.43 ohms.
items.source: Honors Concept 3 Notes - Circuits.pptx, slides 11 and 13

items.title: Magnetism
items.fact: Magnetism is a property of some materials that allows them to give off an attractive or repulsive force. A magnet is a material that gives off an external magnetic field, making it possible to apply a force over a distance.

items.title: Magnetic Force
items.fact: Magnetic force can be attractive or repulsive. The attractive force decreases as magnets get farther apart. Magnetic force is strongest at the magnetic poles, called north and south.

items.title: Magnetic Poles
items.fact: Magnetic poles cannot be isolated. If a magnet is cut, each piece still has both a north pole and a south pole.

items.title: Magnetic Field
items.fact: A magnetic field is the external force given off by a magnet. It is strongest at the poles and allows magnets to apply force over a distance. Field direction is always north to south.

items.title: Magnetic Attraction and Repulsion
items.fact: In magnetism, opposite poles attract and like poles repel. Magnetic fields can create either attractive or repulsive force.

items.title: Earth's Magnetic Field
items.fact: Earth acts like a giant bar magnet because its core is made of metals. This is why Earth has north and south poles. A compass points north because its needle is attracted to Earth's magnetic south pole, which is near geographic north.

items.title: Magnetic Domains
items.fact: All atoms have the potential to be magnetic because of electrons. In magnetic elements, atoms behave like tiny magnets with north and south poles aligned to make a magnetic field. In nonmagnetic elements, domains are not aligned, so there is no apparent magnetism.

items.title: Ferromagnetism
items.fact: Ferromagnetism is when objects placed in a strong magnetic field become magnetized because the poles within their atoms align. This is how materials like iron, cobalt, and nickel can become attracted to magnets.

items.title: Electricity and Magnetism
items.fact: Moving electric charge, or current, can produce a magnetic field. The stronger the current, the stronger the magnetic field that can be produced.

items.title: Electromagnet
items.fact: An electromagnet is a temporary magnet made by placing a piece of iron inside a current-carrying coil of wire. When the switch is open, no current flows and the nail is not magnetic. When the switch is closed, current flows and the nail becomes magnetic.

items.title: Electromagnet Strength
items.fact: An electromagnet becomes stronger when the current is stronger or when there are more loops of wire.

items.title: Electric Motor
items.fact: An electric motor is a device that uses an electromagnet to convert electrical energy into mechanical energy.

items.title: Generator
items.fact: A generator is a device that uses an electromagnet to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy.
